title: Langue de chat
subtitle: Thin, crisp piped French biscuits
excerpt: Langue de chat are delicate piped biscuits made from a simple batter of butter, icing sugar, egg whites, and flour. They bake pale in the centre with golden edges and are dry and crisp throughout.
sourceNotes: Langue de chat (cat's tongue) biscuits appear in nineteenth-century French patisserie manuals as a standard household and confectionery item. The form is documented in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), and in Urbain Dubois, La Cuisine de Tous les Pays (1868). The method is consistent across sources: a light creamed batter piped into finger shapes, baked until just coloured at the edges.

recipe.batchNotes: Bake in batches of 12 to 15; watch closely as they colour fast.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to a week. They lose their crispness in damp conditions.

content.text: The batter is thin and spreads as it bakes, so piped fingers need to be no more than 8 cm long and well spaced on the tray. The timing window is narrow: a minute too short and they are pale and doughy, a minute too long and they colour past gold into brown. Watch the oven from the 7-minute mark and rotate the tray if needed. They firm up from soft to crisp within 2 minutes of coming out.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and icing sugar together until pale and smooth. Add the egg whites one at a time, beating well after each. Stir in the vanilla. Sift the flour over the batter and fold in gently until smooth. The batter should be thick enough to hold a piped shape but still flow slowly.

Transfer the batter to a piping bag fitted with a 1 cm plain nozzle. Pipe fingers about 7 cm long onto the prepared trays, spacing them 4 cm apart to allow for spreading. Bake for 8 to 10 minutes, until the edges are golden and the centres are pale gold. Cool on the trays for 2 minutes before moving to a rack; they are fragile when hot. Store in an airtight tin once completely cold.
